key_points: Electric current is defined as the flow of electric charge per unit time through a conductor
Potential difference between two points drives electric current in a circuit
Ohm’s law establishes the relationship between current, voltage, and resistance
Resistance of a conductor depends on its length, cross-sectional area, and nature of material
In series circuits the same current flows through all components
In parallel circuits the potential difference across each component is the same
Electrical energy consumed in appliances is measured in kilowatt-hour (kWh)
examples: If voltage across a resistor is doubled while resistance remains constant, current also doubles
In a series combination of three resistors, total resistance is the sum of individual resistances
In parallel combination of resistors, total resistance is less than the smallest resistor
Electric heaters convert electrical energy into heat energy
A 100 W bulb consumes more electrical energy than a 60 W bulb when used for the same time
formulas: Resistance of a conductor → R = ρL/A
Series resistance → R = R1 + R2 + R3
Electric power → P = VI = I²R = V²/R
exam_tips: Solve numerical problems involving Ohm’s law and resistance combinations
Understand the derivation of resistance in series and parallel circuits
Memorize all formulas related to electric power and electrical energy
Pay attention to unit conversions such as watt to kilowatt and joule to kilowatt-hour
Practice reasoning questions related to household wiring and appliance power ratings
